key_points: An angle is formed by two rays meeting at a common point called the vertex
Angles are measured in degrees using instruments such as a protractor
Angles are classified as acute, right, obtuse, straight and reflex
Complementary angles add up to 90° while supplementary angles add up to 180°
Adjacent angles share a common vertex and side
A linear pair is a pair of adjacent angles whose sum is 180°
When two lines intersect, vertically opposite angles are equal
Parallel lines are lines in the same plane that never intersect
A transversal is a line that intersects two or more lines
Transversals create different angle pairs such as corresponding and alternate angles
examples: Corresponding angles example when parallel lines are cut by a transversal
Alternate interior angles example in parallel line diagrams
formulas: Interior angles on same side of transversal → Sum = 180° (parallel lines)
exam_tips: Always check if angles form a linear pair when solving problems
Vertically opposite angles are equal even if the diagram looks complex
Use the property of angles around a point (360°) in problem solving
Check if corresponding or alternate interior angles are equal to identify parallel lines
Draw neat diagrams to understand angle relationships clearly
